scrollHero.title: Recovery Takes Time. But It Has Begun.
scrollHero.heading: The Glashapullagh Project
scrollHero.paragraph1: The Glashapullagh site is a drained and cutover blanket bog in West Limerick. This has led to severe peat compaction, the loss of peat and the spread of rushes. To reverse this degradation, a comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed involving drone and ground surveys to map vegetation, peat depth, slope, and drain dimensions.
scrollHero.paragraph2: Approved by the National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS), the restoration works at Glashapullagh were planned with clear objectives: to slow carbon loss by preventing the oxidation of dry peat; to improve habitats for wildlife particularly for ground-nesting birds; and to encourage the return of Sphagnum mosses, by raising the local water table.
scrollHero.paragraph3: The project serves as both a working restoration site and as a model for blanket bog recovery across the Atlantic seaboard. Every technique deployed from timber dams to geotextile to the removal of self-sown conifers has been carefully matched to the site’s specific ecological and hydrological conditions. All of this is available to the visitor to the site but also through digitally through short films and augmented reality tours. We hope the story of this bog will be both resource and an inspiration for all those working on other restoration projects.

hero.title: Glashapullagh
hero.paragraph1: The Glashapullagh site is a drained and cutover blanket bog in West Limerick. This has led to severe peat compaction, the loss of peat and the spread of rushes. To reverse this degradation, a comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed involving drone and ground surveys to map vegetation, peat depth, slope, and drain dimensions.
hero.paragraph2: Approved by the National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS), the restoration works at Glashapullagh were planned with clear objectives: to slow carbon loss by preventing the oxidation of dry peat; to improve habitats for wildlife particularly for ground-nesting birds; and to encourage the return of Sphagnum mosses, by raising the local water table.
hero.paragraph3: The project serves as both a working restoration site and as a model for blanket bog recovery across the Atlantic seaboard. Every technique deployed from timber dams to geotextile to the removal of self-sown conifers has been carefully matched to the site's specific ecological and hydrological conditions. All of this is available to the visitor to the site but also through digitally through short films and augmented reality tours. We hope the story of this bog will be both resource and an inspiration for all those working on other restoration projects.

items.title: Improve Habitats
items.description: Protecting and enhancing habitats for ground-nesting birds including Snipe, and Hen Harrier by restoring the vegetation of the bog and removing self-sown conifers that facilitate predators.

items.title: Restore Peat-Forming Plants
items.description: Encouraging the return of Sphagnum mosses and native bog vegetation by raising the local water table and stabilising exposed peat surfaces. This is the only way to secure the bogs future.

items.title: Rewet the Landscape
items.description: Blocking historic drainage channels using timber dams, peat plugs, composite dams, stone dams, and coir logs to restore the bog's natural hydrology. There is no bog without water, by retaining water on the bog for longer we restore the site but also slow the runoff into rivers after intense rainfall reducing erosion and flood risks.

items.title: Slow Carbon Loss
items.description: Preventing the oxidation of dry peat and loss of Carbon dioxide to the atmosphere by raising the water table. Reducing the loss of dissolved organic carbon and Particulate Carbon by reducing erosion. Ultimately turning a degraded bog from a major Carbon source into a small Carbon sink.

items.title: Tell the Story of a Recovering Bog
items.description: While the site looked a bit messy in the first few months after the works were completed, We don't have to wait long for life to come back. Frogs spawning in the pools, wintering snipe feeding in the soft mud. By the first summer vegetation will start to cover the bare peat, insects will colonise the pools. We will monitor and share news as life returns.

items.title: Thinking Ahead
items.description: Glashapullagh is a small site, but it does serve to demonstrate the positives that restoration delivers. The restoration of a blanket bog does not mean flooding, but it does mean managing the bog for the future. Restoring its hydrology, securing the remaining peat and helping bring back the natural bog vegetation that will in time create new peat.

items.label: of bare peat protected with geotextiles

parallaxBreaks.title: A Landscape in Recovery
parallaxBreaks.text: The Glashapullagh site is a cutover blanket bog that had been historically drained, leading to severe peat compaction, the spread of scrub and rushes, and significant peat loss. A comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed using extensive drone and ground surveys to reverse this degradation.

parallaxBreaks.title: Recovery Takes Time
parallaxBreaks.text: But at Glashapullagh, it has begun. Water is being held on the site for longer, the peat is not flooding, it is rewetting, like a sponge it is soaking up water and expanding. The bog is healing — its hydrology is recovering, the loss of Carbon is slowing, frogs are spawning, Snipe are coming here to feed. Soon, the pools will support swarms of insects, Meadow Pipits and perhaps Snipe will start to breed, and the Hen Harrier will hunt here again. As the years pass the rushes will diminish, Sphagnum will spread and in time new peat will begin to form.

about.label: About
about.title: The Glashapullagh Project
paragraphs: The Glashapullagh site is a drained and cutover blanket bog in West Limerick. This has led to severe peat compaction, the loss of peat and the spread of rushes. To reverse this degradation, a comprehensive Restoration Action Plan was developed involving drone and ground surveys to map vegetation, peat depth, slope, and drain dimensions.

Approved by the National Parks and Wildlife Service (NPWS), the restoration works at Glashapullagh were planned with clear objectives: to slow carbon loss by preventing the oxidation of dry peat; to improve habitats for wildlife particularly for ground-nesting birds; and to encourage the return of Sphagnum mosses, by raising the local water table.

The project serves as both a working restoration site and as a model for blanket bog recovery across the Atlantic seaboard. Every technique deployed from timber dams to geotextile to the removal of self-sown conifers has been carefully matched to the site's specific ecological and hydrological conditions. All of this is available to the visitor to the site but also through digitally through short films and augmented reality tours. We hope the story of this bog will be both resource and an inspiration for all those working on other restoration projects.

topoMap.label: Location
topoMap.title: Topographical Overview
topoMap.description: Interactive map of the Glashapullagh restoration site in West Limerick

items.title: Water Management & Drain Blocking
items.description: Drains dug into the bog previously caused rapid water loss. To rewet the site and remove the drainage gradient, several damming techniques were deployed: timber dams in shallow drains, peat plugs in deep drains, composite wood-peat dams on sloping ground, stone dams where peat was thin, and coir logs to block gullies and raise the water table.

items.title: Peat Stabilisation & Reprofiling
items.description: Decades of peat extraction left behind steep, exposed peat banks (hags) that rapidly dry out, crack, and release carbon. Steep faces were graded into gentle slopes to reduce air exposure and improve water retention. On exposed bare peat, biodegradable jute netting was secured with wooden pins to shield the surface, prevent erosion, and create optimal conditions for moss establishment.

items.title: Habitat & Vegetation Management
items.description: Self-sown Sitka spruce from nearby plantations were removed. Conifers draw water from the peat, cast shade that limits Sphagnum growth, and provide perches for opportunistic predators like Grey Crows that threaten ground-nesting birds like Curlew, Snipe, and Hen Harrier. Seedlings were pulled by hand, while larger saplings were cut and left to safely decay.

items.title: Site Logistics & Waste Management
items.description: All operations were strictly planned to respect the fragile ground. Materials were stored close to the site and transported carefully to minimise surface damage. Legacy waste — including dumped plastic bags, tyres, pallets, and water tanks — was responsibly removed to protect wildlife and safeguard water quality.

items.title: Stone Dams
items.description: Stone dams are used where the drain has eroded down to the mineral layer. They slow the flow of water preventing further erosion and widening of the drain. On this site round stones were used as the slope is shallow and water velocities are low. Where peak flows are stronger, key the dam into the banks and use angular stones that will lock together.

items.title: Coir Logs
items.description: Biodegradable rolls made from coconut husks used to block gullies, dam small drains, and act as bunds in rush-dominated areas. All these help to raise the water table and encourage moss growth. Coir logs must be dug into the peat to prevent water from flowing underneath them. Good choice for repairing small gullies and where access for machines is difficult.

items.title: Geotextiles
items.description: Biodegradable jute netting laid on exposed bare peat and secured with wooden pins. Shields peat from heavy rain, prevents erosion, stabilises the surface, and retains surface moisture helping mosses and plants to establish. Can be used with a mulch of straw or Heather brash.

items.title: Composite Peat/Timber Dams
items.description: Used where there is a risk of timber dams failing due to high water pressure during peak flows. Larch planks keyed into the banks of the drain and reinforced with timber piles driven deep into the drain base. A peat plug is placed upslope from the frame, a second plug can also be applied on the downslope side. The timber framework provides the strength and stability; the compacted peat provides the seal holding the water back. Used on drains up to 2.5m with gentle gradients, anything larger or where peak flows are potentially very strong seek engineering advice.

items.title: Timber Dams
items.description: Used in shallow drains where there is still a thick layer of peat at the base of the drain. Untreated larch planks embedded deeply into shallow drains, with a small notch to direct water safely. Notch controls the depth of the pool, preventing water from flowing around or over the dam. Place a stone or timber "shoe" beneath the notch to break the fall of water and limit erosion. Longer lasting than Coir logs, useful where machinery access is not possible or where only a small number of dams are required. Expected to last 8–10 years while vegetation fills the drains naturally.

whyLarch.label: Materials
whyLarch.title: Why Larch
paragraphs: Larch is a fast-growing deciduous conifer; it is grown in Ireland (24,000 ha in 2021) so availability is good and there are less transport issues than coir. Untreated Larch timber has natural resins that allow the wood to resist decay. Most other woods are either too expensive or having limited availability, such as Oak, or decay like White Deal. Using treated timbers introduces long lasting toxic chemicals to the bog which we would prefer not to do.

If Larch planks are embedded in the peat as in a composite peat/timber dam they will last for decades. If they are exposed to the air as with a timber dam and subject to wet/dry cycles they will not endure as long. They will still outlast coir and hold long enough for the pools behind them to fill in and revegetate. By the time the Larch planks break down, they should no longer be needed.
Larch was traditionally used for the hulls of lake boats used for angling on our lakes.

items.title: Overview
items.description: An overview of the project, landscape, and restoration vision for Glashapullagh.

items.title: Timber Dams
items.description: Installing untreated larch plank dams in shallow drains to slow water flow and re-wet the bog.

items.title: Peat Plugs
items.description: Packing native peat into deep drains to block drainage and raise the water table.

items.title: Composite Wood-Peat Dams
items.description: Larch planks reinforced with timber posts for wide or deep drains on sloping ground.

items.title: Stone Dams
items.description: Non-calcareous stone placed where little peat remains, protecting the bog's water chemistry.

items.title: Coir Logs
items.description: Biodegradable coconut husk rolls blocking gullies and small drains to raise the water table.

items.title: Geotextiles
items.description: Biodegradable jute netting protecting exposed bare peat and accelerating moss establishment.

items.title: Removing Conifers
items.description: Hand-removing self-sown Sitka spruce to restore open, wet conditions and protect wildlife.

items.title: Reprofiling Peat Banks
items.description: Grading steep hags into gentle slopes to reduce air exposure and stabilise vulnerable peat.

items.title: Waste Management
items.description: Identifying, sorting, and responsibly removing legacy waste to protect wildlife and water quality.

items.title: Logistics and Safety
items.description: Planning materials, safe working practices, and emergency procedures on fragile bogland.
